# Practical Strategies for Embedding Design Thinking in Your Organization

![Abby](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*ikRTF6lMyIYZDpB15RPY0g@2x.jpeg)

Design thinking, often hailed as a transformative approach to problem-solving, has become a buzzword in corporate strategy and innovation discussions, touted as a panacea for innovation and problem-solving and [crisis management](/navigating-crisis-with-design-thinking-9eeb6e8910d4) . From Silicon Valley startups to Fortune 500 companies, organizations are rushing to adopt this [human-centered approach to creativity.](/bridging-creativity-and-structure-integrating-design-thinking-into-project-management-e4df52662214) While its principles – empathy, ideation, prototyping, and iteration – offer tremendous value, organizations frequently struggle to move beyond surface-level adoption. The challenge lies in embedding design thinking as a core organizational capability rather than treating it as a one-off workshop or temporary trend. This article critically examines the challenges of implementing design thinking and offers practical strategies for organizations to move beyond buzzwords and achieve meaningful impact.

# **The Promise and Pitfalls of Design Thinking**

Design thinking promises to revolutionize how organizations approach problems by prioritizing empathy, collaboration, and experimentation. Its five-stage framework – empathize, define, ideate, prototype, and test – offers a structured yet flexible process for tackling complex challenges. However, the reality often falls short of the promise. Many organizations treat design thinking as a one-off workshop or a superficial add-on rather than a fundamental shift in mindset and operations. The pitfalls of design thinking implementation are numerous:

Many organizations view design thinking as a quick fix to innovation challenges. Leaders enthusiastically launch workshops with post-it notes, brainstorming sessions, and flashy prototypes, expecting radical outcomes. However, without structural changes and sustained commitment, these efforts often fizzle out, leading to cynicism among employees who see design thinking as just another management fad.

A critical mistake is assuming that design thinking is solely a toolset rather than a mindset. To truly embed it within the organization, leaders must foster a culture that values curiosity, experimentation, and continuous learning. This involves not just teaching techniques but reshaping how teams approach problem-solving at every level.

# **Practical Strategies for Embedding Design Thinking**

**1. Leadership Buy-In and Cultural Shift:** For design thinking to take root, leadership must model its principles by embracing empathy, customer-centricity, and experimentation. Leaders should create safe spaces for teams to test ideas without fear of failure. Design thinking cannot thrive without active support from the top. Leaders must not only endorse the approach but also embody its principles. This means:

Leadership commitment is often the missing link in design thinking efforts. Without it, initiatives are doomed to remain superficial and short-lived.

**2. Cross-Functional Collaboration:** Design thinking thrives on diverse perspectives. Breaking down organizational silos and encouraging cross-functional collaboration can generate innovative solutions by bringing varied insights to complex problems. To sustain design thinking efforts, organizations must demonstrate their value. This requires:

Measuring the impact of design thinking can be challenging, as its benefits are often intangible and long-term. Organizations must avoid oversimplifying metrics and focus on qualitative as well as quantitative outcomes.

**3. Empathy as a Core Value:** Organizations need to move beyond lip service to empathy. This means spending time with customers, understanding their pain points, and involving them in co-creation processes. Design thinking is rooted in empathy – understanding the needs and perspectives of users. Organizations must foster a culture that values collaboration and cross-functional teamwork. Strategies include:

• Creating multidisciplinary teams to tackle challenges.

• Encouraging open communication and active listening.

• Rewarding behaviors that align with design thinking principles, such as curiosity and experimentation.

Building such a culture requires time and effort. Organizations must be prepared to address resistance and invest in long-term cultural change.

**4. Embedding Design Thinking in Processes:** Design thinking should be integrated into key business processes, from product development to customer service. Establishing measurable frameworks that assess customer experience and innovation outcomes helps sustain its impact. Design thinking should not exist in a silo. To maximize its impact, organizations must integrate it into their existing workflows and decision-making processes. This can be achieved by:

[• Incorporating design thinking stages into project management frameworks.](/bridging-creativity-and-structure-integrating-design-thinking-into-project-management-e4df52662214)

• Using design thinking to inform strategic planning and product development.

• Aligning design thinking initiatives with key performance indicators (KPIs) and organizational goals.

Integration requires careful planning and alignment. Organizations must avoid forcing design thinking into processes where it doesn’t fit, as this can lead to frustration and disengagement.

**5. Capability Building:** Investing in training programs that go beyond introductory workshops is essential. Teams should be equipped with the skills and mindset needed to apply design thinking to real-world problems regularly. Design thinking is a skill that must be learned and practiced. Step taken should include:

• Offering workshops and hands-on training sessions.

• Providing access to design thinking resources, such as books, case studies, and online courses.

• Creating opportunities for employees to apply their learning in real-world projects.

Training alone is not enough. Organizations must create an environment where employees feel empowered to apply their new skills and take risks.

# Conclusion

Design thinking is more than a trendy buzzword – it is a powerful approach to innovation and problem-solving. However, its potential can only be realized if organizations move beyond superficial adoption and embed it into their culture, processes, and strategy. This requires leadership commitment, cultural change, and a willingness to experiment and learn.

By embracing design thinking as a core business imperative, organizations can unlock new opportunities, drive meaningful innovation, and create solutions that truly meet the needs of their users. The journey is not easy, but the rewards are worth the effort. In a world of constant change and complexity, design thinking offers a path forward – but only if we are willing to walk it.

Design thinking is a creative problem-solving approach that puts human needs at the center of innovation. It encourages you to think from the user’s perspective and to develop solutions that are not only functional but also intuitive and delightful. This mindset involves understanding users, challenging assumptions, and redefining problems to identify alternative strategies and solutions.

The essence of design thinking can be broken down into several key phases, commonly known as the [design thinking framework](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-framework). These phases include:

Adopting a design thinking mindset means engaging in these phases iteratively and flexibly, always ready to loop back and adjust your approach based on what you learn.

### Benefits of Adopting a Design Thinking Mindset

Embracing the design thinking mindset offers numerous advantages, particularly for innovation professionals looking to develop new products, services, customer experiences, and business models. Here are some key benefits:

| Benefit | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Enhanced Creativity | Encourages thinking beyond conventional limits. |
| Improved User Experience | Focuses on addressing real user needs. |
| Collaborative Approach | Integrates diverse perspectives and fosters teamwork. |
| Reduces Risk | Early testing and iteration help in identifying and correcting issues early. |
| Accelerates Innovation | Streamlines the process from ideation to execution. |

By adopting a design thinking mindset, you can create more innovative, user-friendly, and successful products and services. This approach equips you with the tools and [design thinking principles](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-principles) necessary to navigate the complexities of modern innovation projects effectively. For further insights into how design thinking can be applied, explore our article on [design thinking user experience](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-user-experience).

## Cultivating a Design Thinking Mindset

Adopting a design thinking mindset is crucial for innovation professionals looking to develop new products, services, and customer experiences. Here are some key aspects to focus on.

### Openness to New Ideas and Perspectives

Being open to new ideas and perspectives is at the core of a design thinking mindset. This involves stepping out of your comfort zone and being willing to explore uncharted territories. It’s essential to embrace diverse viewpoints and consider all ideas, no matter how unconventional they may seem.

| Aspect | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Diverse Teams | Include members from various backgrounds and domains. |
| Brainstorming Sessions | Encourage free flow of ideas without judgment. |
| User Feedback | Actively seek feedback from end-users at every stage. |

Exploring new perspectives can lead to ground-breaking solutions. Incorporate brainstorming sessions and encourage team members to share their unique insights. User feedback is also invaluable; it provides real-world perspectives that can significantly influence your design thinking process. For more insights on this approach, visit our page on [human-centered design thinking](https://sorenkaplan.com/human-centered-design-thinking).

### Embracing Iteration and Experimentation

Iteration and experimentation are fundamental to design thinking. This involves continuously refining and testing ideas to achieve the best possible outcome. Instead of fearing failure, view it as an opportunity to learn and improve.

| Stage | Objective |
| --- | --- |
| Ideation | Generate a wide range of ideas. |
| Prototyping | Create simple models to test concepts. |
| Testing | Gather user feedback and refine the idea. |

In the ideation phase, focus on quantity over quality to keep the creative juices flowing. Move on to prototyping by creating simple, workable models of your ideas. Testing those prototypes with end-users allows you to gather valuable feedback and make necessary adjustments. This iterative cycle helps you develop more robust and user-friendly solutions.

Effective design thinking also requires a supportive environment. Encourage a culture where experimentation is celebrated, and learning from failures is valued. For more techniques on fostering this mindset, check out our article on [design thinking workshop activities](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-workshop-activities).

By integrating these practices, you can cultivate a design thinking mindset that enhances your ability to innovate and create impactful solutions. Adopting a continuous learning and adaptive approach can sustain your journey into design thinking. For further guidance, explore our resources on [design thinking strategies](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-strategies).

## Applying Design Thinking in Practice

Moving from theory to practice, applying a design thinking mindset to your projects involves hands-on methods that put the user at the center of the solution. Here’s how you can do it effectively.

### Empathy and Human-Centered Design

Empathy is the cornerstone of the [design thinking methodology](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-methodology). Understanding the needs, desires, and challenges of your users allows you to create solutions that truly resonate with them.

This process, known as human-centered design, ensures that the solutions you create are both relevant and meaningful. For more on the concept, see our article on [human-centered design thinking](https://sorenkaplan.com/human-centered-design-thinking).

### Prototyping and Testing Ideas

Once you have a deep understanding of your users, the next step is to translate these insights into tangible solutions through prototyping and testing.

**Prototyping**: Create simple, scalable versions of your ideas. These can range from sketches and storyboards to physical models and digital mockups. The purpose is to visualize your ideas quickly and affordably.

**Testing**:

| Phase | Key Activities | Objective |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Prototyping | Sketching, Building Models | Visualize and Communicate Ideas |
| Testing | User Feedback, Observations | Validate and Refine Ideas |
| Iteration | Feedback Implementation | Improve Usability and Functionality |

Understanding these principles is crucial. For more detailed strategies, read our guide on [design thinking strategies](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-strategies).

Applying a design thinking mindset also means fostering an environment where empathy, experimentation, and collaboration thrive. Whether it’s for developing new products, services, or business models, practicing these principles will elevate your approach to innovation. For more workshop activities, explore our suggestions on [design thinking workshop activities](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-workshop-activities).

## Sustaining Your Design Thinking Journey

### Continuous Learning and Adaptation

To maintain and enhance your design thinking mindset, continuous learning and adaptation are key. It’s essential to stay curious and keep expanding your knowledge. Engage in activities, workshops, and courses that delve into the nuances of the [design thinking framework](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-framework). You can also participate in communities and discussions that focus on [design thinking principles](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-principles) and practices.

One effective way to measure your progress is through ongoing reflection and feedback. Regularly assess your approach and outcomes to identify areas for improvement. Embrace feedback from colleagues, clients, and users as a valuable source of learning.

| Activity | Frequency | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Workshops | Monthly | Hands-on experience and networking |
| Feedback Sessions | Bi-Weekly | Gather insights and improve processes |
| Reading Articles | Weekly | Stay updated on trends and best practices |
| Courses | Quarterly | Deepen understanding of specific areas |

Additionally, experimenting with different aspects of the [design thinking methodology](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-methodology) can reveal new insights and foster innovation. Don’t hesitate to try uncharted paths and new tools to see how they might enhance your projects.

### Building a Collaborative and Creative Environment

Creating an environment that encourages collaboration and creativity is essential for sustaining a design thinking mindset. Foster a workplace culture where team members feel safe sharing ideas, regardless of how unconventional they may seem. Emphasize the importance of teamwork and collective problem-solving, rooted in [human-centered design thinking](https://sorenkaplan.com/human-centered-design-thinking).

Organize regular [design thinking workshop activities](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-workshop-activities) to practice brainstorming, prototyping, and testing in a collaborative setting. This not only strengthens team bonds but also sparks creativity.

| Element | Action | Benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Collaborative Spaces | Set up areas for team collaboration | Encourages open dialogue and idea sharing |
| Brainstorming Sessions | Schedule weekly idea sessions | Boosts collective problem solving |
| Open Feedback Culture | Implement regular feedback loops | Enhances continuous improvement |
| Team Activities | Plan regular team-building exercises | Strengthens trust and cooperation |

Encourage an atmosphere where experimentation is welcomed, and “failures” are celebrated as learning opportunities. By doing so, you create a resilient team capable of innovative thinking and adaptive strategies.

Fostering a dynamic environment rooted in continuous learning and adaptation, along with a robust collaborative culture, ensures your design thinking journey remains vibrant and productive. Leverage these practices to maximize the impact of your innovation efforts and develop groundbreaking products, services, and experiences. For more techniques on nurturing a design thinking mindset, explore our articles on [design thinking for social innovation](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-for-social-innovation) and [design thinking leadership](https://sorenkaplan.com/design-thinking-leadership).

URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197

SUMMARY:
Unlike traditional innovation management frameworks such as stage-gate and waterfall, which rely on high-fidelity testing at the end of a project to validate results before launch (Paluch et al., 2019), modern approaches such as design thinking, agile development, lean startup, and growth hacking (Mansoori and Lackeus, 2020; Blank and Eckhardt, 2023; Cavallo et al., 2024) integrate experimentation for exploratory learning throughout the innovation process (Shaik et al., 2023; Yi et al., 2022). While qualitative studies have highlighted the importance of rapid prototyping and user engagement (e.g., Liedtka et al., 2024), quantitative research examining the direct impact of design thinking practices – and experimentation in particular – on innovation outcomes remains scarce (Nagaraj et al., 2020; Nakata and Hwang, 2020; Magistretti et al., 2022a; Robbins and Fu, 2022).

Highlights
----------

*   •Experimentation practices and digital technologies are recognized as germane for design thinking projects. 
*   •Early and frequent experimentation practices are positively related to innovation effectiveness. 
*   •Early experimentation practices are positively related to project efficiency. 

Abstract
--------

Design thinking is an innovation approach that emphasizes developing and testing hypotheses about the desirability, feasibility, and viability of an idea through iterative experimentation. Although widely used, there is limited empirical evidence to support the effectiveness of experimentation practices in design thinking projects. Similarly, the impact of integrating digital technologies into experimentation processes remains underexplored. This study addresses these gaps by analyzing data from 246 design thinking projects to examine how early and frequent experimentation influences innovation performance, specifically in terms of effectiveness and efficiency. It also examines how the use of digital technologies moderates these relationships. The results show that both early and frequent experimentation positively influence innovation effectiveness, while only early experimentation significantly improves innovation efficiency. Moreover, the use of digital technologies strengthens the positive effects of early experimentation on both effectiveness and efficiency. This research provides valuable theoretical and practical insights by deepening our understanding of how experimentation and digital tools drive innovation performance in design thinking projects.

*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000173)

Keywords
--------

Experimentation

Innovation

Design thinking

Prototyping

Digital technologies

1. Introduction
---------------

Innovation methodologies such as design thinking ([Magistretti et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib74); [Carlgren and Ben Mahmoud-Jouini, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib22)), agile development ([Bianchi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib11)), lean startup ([Ries, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib99); [Shepherd and Gruber, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib108)), and growth hacking ([Cavallo et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib28)), have gained significant traction among both academics and practitioners. Despite their different principles and practices, these approaches share a fundamental reliance on experimentation ([Mansoori and Lackeus, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib79)). The concept of experimentation has been explored in various disciplines, including strategy (e.g., [Nicholls-Nixon et al., 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib90)) and entrepreneurship (e.g., [Lindholm-Dahlstrand et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib70)). In the context of innovation, experimentation serves to validate the core assumptions behind novel ideas by assessing their desirability, feasibility, and viability ([Brown, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib17); [Thomke, 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib112); [Hampel et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib55)). This process typically involves the use of prototypes – early, imperfect representations of the innovation ([Beltagui et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib7)) – to actively engage with and modify real-world scenarios ([Bogers et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib13)). By testing these prototypes with target users, innovators can create realistic simulations of potential products or services and generate valuable user feedback ([Pisano, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib96)). This feedback is essential for making informed design and strategic decisions throughout the development process. According to [Bogers and Horst (2014)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib14), experimentation is characterized by “active engagement, concrete intervention, and selective encounter of reality through prototypes”. Active engagement fosters continuous interaction between innovators and end users, which enhances the iterative learning process ([Magnusson, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib78)). Prototypes can create a vivid experience, a tangible preview of future innovations, and elicit authentic user responses ([Pisano, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib96)). When conducted early and often, these encounters with simplified models of reality provide critical insights that strengthen decision-making as innovation projects evolve ([Magistretti et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib74)). In environments characterized by uncertainty and complexity ([Howard-Grenville, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib58)), experimentation helps organizations interpret their context and set the strategic direction ([Furr and Eisenhardt, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib46)). For example, system dynamics modeling can simulate the impact of strategic and organizational changes on business models, allowing firms to anticipate potential outcomes ([Cosenz and Noto, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib34)). By leveraging this dynamic approach, firms gain a deeper understanding of how external factors shape decisions, making experimentation an essential tool for evaluating and refining strategies prior to full implementation.

Experimentation has become central to innovation processes as its adoption and importance continue to grow ([Watanabe and Tou, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib122)), with an increasing focus on experimentation as a key practice ([Hampel et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib55); [Thomke, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib113); [Sanasi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib105)). It is now widely implemented across industries ([Pisano, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib96)), firm functions ([Spear and Bowen, 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib110); [Petersen and Wohlin, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib94)), and firm contexts, from startups to large established companies ([Felin et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib42); [Sanasi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib106)) and consulting firms ([Magistretti et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib75)). At companies such as Airbnb and [Booking.com](http://booking.com/), designing and running thousands of experiments is a core, routine process ([Verganti et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib119)).

Unlike traditional innovation management frameworks such as stage-gate and waterfall, which rely on high-fidelity testing at the end of a project to validate results before launch ([Paluch et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib92)), modern approaches such as design thinking, agile development, lean startup, and growth hacking ([Mansoori and Lackeus, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib79); [Blank and Eckhardt, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib12); [Cavallo et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib28)) integrate experimentation for exploratory learning throughout the innovation process ([Shaik et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib107); [Yi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib126)). This continuous experimentation is particularly important for innovation projects characterized by high levels of ambiguity ([Liedtka, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib66); [Magistretti et al., 2022b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib77)).

Design thinking has gained widespread adoption and prominence in organizations ranging from consulting firms, such as Deloitte Digital, PwC, and Accenture ([Dell’Era et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib35)), to established corporations such as PepsiCo and 3M ([Gemser et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib51)). Recognized as an effective approach to product and service innovation ([Magistretti et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib76)), design thinking is widely accepted as a formal method for creative problem solving that is particularly well suited to addressing ill-defined problems ([Brown, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib17)). Central to design thinking is the ability to navigate ambiguity, making it particularly useful for tackling “wicked problems” ([Buchanan, 1992](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib18); [Rittel and Webber, 1973](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib100)). [Verganti et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib118) highlight iterative prototyping as a defining characteristic of design thinking. This approach involves learning through trial and error, frequent user involvement, and the use of visual and material representations to explore potential solutions. Rooted in experimentation ([Bogers et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib13)), design thinking fundamentally relies on iterative testing ([Elsbach and Stigliani, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib41); [Klenner et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib62)). For example, the development of “quick and dirty” prototypes allows teams to explore the solution space and identify the most promising innovation opportunities ([Stigliani and Ravasi, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib111); [Ben Mahmoud-Jouini and Midler, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib9)). In addition, experimentation in design thinking is critical during the definition phase, where it helps reframe problems ([Pham et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib95)). Crafting problem statements and transforming them into “how might we” questions allow design thinkers to better understand and address complex problems through continuous, iterative exploration ([Durante et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib38)). Indeed, the initial problem statement in design thinking serves as a preliminary hypothesis that is subsequently tested to assess its robustness and value. This process naturally leads to reframing the problem similar to experimenting with artifacts (i.e., problem formulation) in real-world interactions ([Bogers et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib13)). Furthermore, experimentation is widely recognized as essential during the prototyping phase to test ideas ([Liedtka, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib66)). This aligns with designers' iterative approach to identifying and testing assumptions about problems and developing tangible solutions ([Micheli et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib84)). As such, experimentation is integral to every stage of the design thinking process, both early ([Durante et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib38)) and late ([Liedtka, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib66)). Despite the growing adoption of experimentation within design thinking and its recognized importance in driving innovation ([Hampel et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib55); [Thomke, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib113)), there is still a lack of systematic evidence detailing how experimentation specifically contributes to innovation performance in design thinking contexts. While qualitative studies have highlighted the importance of rapid prototyping and user engagement (e.g., [Liedtka et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib69)), quantitative research examining the direct impact of design thinking practices – and experimentation in particular – on innovation outcomes remains scarce ([Nagaraj et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib87); [Nakata and Hwang, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib88); [Magistretti et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib75); [Robbins and Fu, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib101)). Recent literature reviews have highlighted the need for empirical validation of the impact of design thinking on measurable innovation outcomes ([Micheli et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib84); [Magistretti et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib74)), yet this gap remains. Given the widespread use of design thinking in practice, its potential impact on innovation performance, and the central role of experimentation in both innovation and design thinking, understanding this relationship is critical. Such insights are valuable not only for scholars seeking to advance innovation theories, but also for practitioners seeking to make informed decisions about investing in experimentation practices. Hence, we pose the following research question.
*   RQ1: How do experimentation practices contribute to innovation performance in design thinking projects? 

In parallel, the rapid advancement and widespread adoption of digital technologies – such as artificial intelligence (AI), big data analytics, and 3D printing – have created new opportunities for early-stage, low-cost prototyping and accelerated user feedback cycles ([Bianchi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib11); [Candi and Beltagui, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib25); [Mariani and Nambisan, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib81)). Recent studies examining the impact of AI on innovation processes show how these technologies enable faster, more affordable experimentation while generating richer consumer insights ([Roberts and Candi, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib102)). As a result, there is growing scholarly interest in exploring how digital technologies are transforming innovation practices. Research has increasingly focused on the role of AI, big data ([Mariani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib80)), 3D printing, the Internet of Things (IoT), and other next-generation digital tools in shaping innovation outcomes ([Cho et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib29)). These studies aim to deepen our understanding of not only how these technologies are used in innovation processes ([Mariani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib80); [Roberts and Candi, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib102)), but also how they can be effectively integrated and leveraged together to improve experimentation and overall innovation performance ([Cho et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib29)). [Liedtka (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib67) envisions a synergistic relationship between design thinking and digital technologies, suggesting that design thinking practices can be enhanced by digital tools, while these technologies can simultaneously support and streamline design thinking processes. Extending this perspective, recent research has highlighted the role of AI, particularly generative AI, in expanding the scope of problem solving and solution generation, thereby accelerating the innovation process ([Bouschery et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib16)). Conversely, empirical studies have shown that big data, while providing valuable insights, may be insufficient to fully support design thinking. Instead, thick data – rich, qualitative insights – remain essential for effective creative problem solving, requiring a balance of both data types ([Mortati et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib86)). Despite these advances, however, there is still a lack of quantitative evidence on how digital technologies influence the relationship between experimentation and innovation performance in design thinking contexts. To address this gap, we propose the following research question.
*   RQ2: How does the use of digital technologies moderate the relationship between experimentation practices and innovation performance in design thinking projects? 

To answer these questions, we analyze quantitative data from 246 innovation projects conducted by consulting firms that use design thinking to solve their clients’ problems. Our results show that early experimentation in design thinking projects is positively associated with both innovation effectiveness and efficiency. Furthermore, the use of digital technologies significantly strengthens these relationships. In contrast, while frequent experimentation is positively associated with innovation effectiveness, it does not enhance innovation efficiency, and the use of digital technologies does not moderate this relationship.

The contributions of this research are threefold. First, it provides important empirical evidence on the effectiveness of experimentation practices in the context of design thinking, directly addressing recent calls for more quantitative research in this area ([Micheli et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib84); [Magistretti et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib74)). This paper also advances our understanding of design thinking as an evidence-based, iterative approach rooted in various forms of experimentation ([Bogers and Horst, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib14); [Thomke, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib113)), rather than a rigid, prescriptive process. By emphasizing the dynamic and exploratory nature of design thinking, this research reinforces the central role of experimentation in driving problem solving and innovation ([Carlgren et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib27); [Liedtka, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib67)). Second, it advances the discourse on experimentation ([Thomke, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib113)) by demonstrating that this is not a singular concept ([Verganti et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib118)), but includes distinct practices, especially early and frequent experimentation, each of which uniquely influences innovation performance. Recognizing this distinction allows for a more nuanced understanding of how different experimentation strategies contribute to innovation outcomes and provides a refined framework for managing experimentation in design thinking processes. Third, this study contributes to the growing discussion on the role of digital technologies in creative processes (i.e., design thinking) ([Liedtka, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib67)) by revealing how digital technologies moderate the relationship between experimentation and innovation performance. This finding highlights the critical role that digital technologies play in enhancing the effectiveness of experimentation and the need for granular, quantitative testing to validate relationships that are often merely hypothesized or assumed. Moreover, by providing actionable insights into how specific experimentation practices affect innovation outcomes, this research offers valuable implications for scholars seeking to deepen theoretical models and practitioners seeking to optimize innovation strategies.

-----------------------------------

### 2.1. Experimentation practices in design thinking projects

Design thinking goes beyond a focus on aesthetics and product form to provide a comprehensive creative problem-solving approach that fosters innovation ([Brown, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib17); [Liedtka et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib68); [Dell’Era et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib35)). It is particularly effective in addressing “wicked” and ill-defined problems by expanding both the scope of the problem and the potential solutions ([Dorst and Cross, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib37); [Foss and Saebi, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib44)), while encouraging creativity among those involved in solving the problem ([Tripp, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib116)). Although design thinking can be interpreted in many ways, it is generally recognized for three core characteristics: a human-centered perspective ([Buchanan, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib19); [Brown, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib17)), the use of creativity to reframe problems ([Beckman and Barry, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib6); [Dorst, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib36); [Beckman, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166497225000197#bib5)), and a strong emphasis on prototyping or experimentation ([Fraser, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S016649

# Improve Your Skills: 10 Top Design Thinking Tips

![Diverse team engaged in design thinking activities](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20640%20360'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

Posted in [Blog](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/category/blog/), [Design Thinking](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/category/design-thinking/), [Facilitation](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/category/blog/facilitation/), [Innovation](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/category/blog/innovation-blogs/), [Virtual Facilitation](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/category/blog/facilitation/virtual-facilitation/) by Jo North

## 10 Lesser-known Design Thinking Tips

In the landscape of problem-solving and product development, Design Thinking has emerged as a potent tool. It helps you and your team to to break free from the status quo and create innovative solutions.

This article explores **10 lesser-known Design Thinking tips** that will take your skills and results to the next level.

You’ll also find my articles on the **[Best Design Thinking Tools](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/design-thinking-tools/)** and **[Design Thinking 101](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/design-thinking-101/)** super useful, too.

## Design Thinking Tip #1: Embracing Uncertainty in the Design Thinking Process

### The Journey of Discovery in Design Thinking

**Design Thinking is fundamentally a journey of discovery. It is an iterative process of exploration and ideation, prototyping and [testing](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/creative-facilitation-techniques-for-validating-business-ideas/) that often leads us in directions we could not have predicted at the outset. This can be daunting, but it is also an integral part of the process. Being open to where the Design Thinking process will take you is key to uncovering innovative solutions.**

Uncertainty can be a powerful driver of [creativity](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/how-to-be-more-creative/). It encourages us to question our assumptions, explore new perspectives, and challenge the status quo. It drives us to ask not just the obvious questions, but the difficult ones – those that force us to think more deeply about our users’ needs and the context in which our solutions will exist.

Mindset is so important , and drives our behaviors, decisions and actions. That’s why it’s top of the list of my Design Thinking tips.

#### Design Thinking Best Practices for Navigating the Unpredictable Design Thinking Journey

##### Leveraging Prototyping and Testing to Navigate Uncertainty

Rapid prototyping and testing are powerful tools for navigating uncertainty. They allow us to experiment with different ideas, [learn from our failures](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/058-the-f-bomb-f-for-failure-in-innovation/), and iterate on our solutions. Rather than seeking to eliminate uncertainty, prototyping and testing enable us to learn from it. It helps us to turn it into a source of insight and innovation.

##### Embracing Failure as a Path to Success

In the Design Thinking process, failure is not a roadblock but a stepping stone towards success. It is through our mistakes that we learn and grow. By embracing failure, we can turn it into a source of insight, learning, and [innovation](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/what-is-business-innovation/).

## Design Thinking Tip #2:The Transformative Power of “Yes, And…”

**[Tom Kelley](https://www.ideo.com/people/tom-kelley), founder of IDEO, champions the [“Yes, And…” improv technique](https://www.amazon.com/Yes-Improvisation-Reverses-Creativity-Collaboration-Lessons/dp/0062248545), where team members build upon each other’s ideas, encouraging a free flow of creative solutions. This technique fosters empathy and collaboration, enhancing the creative process, and propelling possible solutions.**

In the realm of Design Thinking, the “Yes, And” technique is a supercharger for innovative ideas and collaboration. It works by building on each other’s ideas instead of dismissing them. When someone proposes an idea, instead of saying ‘but’, you say ‘Yes, And’ and add something to it.

The magic of ‘Yes, And’ is that it fosters a supportive, inclusive atmosphere that encourages every team member to contribute. By accepting and expanding on each other’s thoughts, we eliminate the fear of judgment and spark an explosion of creativity.

Embracing the ‘Yes, And’ mindset in our Design Thinking sprints has led to [more robust brainstorming sessions](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/brainstorming-activities-for-online-meetings/), unlocking pathways to solutions we might never have discovered otherwise.

Remember, in the ideation phase, there are no bad ideas. Let’s encourage, build, and create. ‘Yes, And’ our way to innovation.

## Design Thinking Tip #3: The Impact of Wild Card Collaborations and Extreme User Design

### Harnessing Diverse Perspectives for Innovative Solutions

**Wild Card Collaborations involve team members from varied backgrounds, driving creative confidence, and fostering innovative ideas. These collaborations challenge assumptions, leading to a more comprehensive list of ideas that pushes the boundaries of conventional thinking.**

Mixing things up, pairing people who wouldn’t ordinarily work together, or collaborating across industries, can lead to truly innovative solutions.

What happens when an engineer brainstorms with a graphic designer? Or when a marketer works closely with a data scientist? What brilliant ideas might emerge when we move beyond our comfort zones? The answers are as thrilling as they are limitless.

Wild Card Collaborations challenge our assumptions, break down silos, and disrupt our typical patterns of thinking. They energise the team by introducing fresh perspectives and different ways of problem-solving. The result? A creative hotpot that often sizzles with unexpected ideas and solutions!

This approach is all about embracing the unfamiliar, the unknown, the ‘wild’ – because that’s where true innovation lives.

Let’s mix it up, challenge norms, and keep surprising ourselves. Dare to go ‘wild’ in your collaborations!

## Design Thinking Tip #4: Extreme User Design: Stretching the Design Process

**Designing for extreme users helps address the needs of people across the spectrum, encouraging us to explore new ways of solving problems. By incorporating extreme users into the design process, we can create more inclusive and effective solutions.**

Extreme Users are the individuals who push the boundaries of typical product or service usage – they may be the most passionate, most demanding, or have unique needs that challenge our conventional design thinking.

So why focus on the edge cases? When we design for extreme users, we uncover needs and desires that might be hidden in the ‘average’ user, leading to solutions that can benefit everyone. It’s about diving deep into the ‘why’ and ‘how’ of their unique interactions.

Extreme user design stretches our creative muscles. It encourages us to consider the full spectrum of experiences and potential solutions, breaking away from the norm. The result? Innovations that are as inclusive as they are groundbreaking!

By embracing the diversity of human experience, we can design products and services that aren’t just for the ‘average’ user, but for ALL users. So let’s dive in, embrace the extremes, and unlock greater creative potential.

## Design Thinking Tip #5: Constraint-Based Design: Unleashing Creativity Within Boundaries

### The Beauty of Boundaries in Design Thinking

**Constraints, as argued by [Tim Brown in the Harvard Business Review](https://hbr.org/2008/06/design-thinking), can indeed be catalysts for creative thinking. Constraint-based design pushes us to find innovative ways to solve complex problems within defined boundaries, sparking creative solutions that resonate deeply with the end user.**

Embracing constraints might seem counter-intuitive, and is one of the least well-known of my Design Thinking tips. It’s actually a powerful tool in design and innovation. Welcome to the world of Constraint-Based Design.

Constraint-Based Design is about using limitations as a springboard for creativity. It could be a limited budget, time, materials, or even specific user needs. Instead of seeing these constraints as hurdles, we use them to guide and inspire our design solutions.

How does it work? When we’re faced with constraints, our problem-solving abilities kick into high gear. We become more focused, more innovative, and often uncover solutions we might not have considered in a limitless space.

Embracing Constraint-Based Design starts with reframing. Look at each constraint as a unique design challenge, a puzzle to solve. Remember, limitations are not roadblocks; they are the contours that shape our creative landscape.

Next time you’re faced with a design problem, don’t shy away from constraints. Embrace them, use them as your guides, and watch your creativity soar!

## Design Thinking Tip #6: Beyond the Product

### Designing Holistic Experiences

**In Design Thinking, we aim to create more than just products or services; we design holistic experiences. We consider not only the product design but also the broader user experience and customer experience, taking into account the emotional, cultural, and environmental contexts. This perspective allows us to design solutions that are empathetic, inclusive, sustainable, and deeply resonant with our users.**

In design thinking, we’re not just designing products or services, we’re designing experiences. We’re looking beyond the tangible, delving into the emotional, cultural, and environmental context in which our solutions exist.

Why is this perspective so crucial? Because it allows us to see the complete picture, to understand our users on a deeper level, and to create solutions that truly resonate. It encourages empathy, inclusivity, and sustainability, elements that are essential in today’s world.

So, how can we think beyond the product?

In our design thinking journey, we are not just creators, we are **[change makers](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/how-to-be-a-change-maker-definitive-guide/)**. Let’s look beyond the product, understand the larger context, and design solutions that make a difference.

## Design Thinking Tip #7: Empathy Mapping

### Diving Deep into the User’s World

**Empathy Mapping, as described by [Jeanne Liedtka in her case studies](https://designatdarden.org/app/uploads/2018/01/Working-paper-Liedtka-Evaluating-the-Impact-of-Design-Thinking.pdf), allows us to explore our users’ needs on a deeper level. This tool** **helps to** **give us a comprehensive understanding of what our users say, think, feel, and do**. **This enables us to design solutions that truly resonate.**

Empathy mapping is an incredible tool in the design thinking toolkit, enabling us to dive deep into our users’ minds, hearts, and lives. It’s not just about understanding what they do, but how they feel, what they see, what they hear, and what they need.

Why is empathy mapping so essential? It illuminates the subtleties of our users’ experiences, unearthing valuable insights that can shape impactful solutions. It’s the cornerstone of human-centred design, pushing us to design for real needs rather than assumptions.

So, how do we create an empathy map? Here’s a simple four-step process:

With empathy mapping, we can tune into our users’ experiences on a deeper level, bringing their voices, emotions, and needs to the forefront of our design process.

## Design Thinking Tip #8: Storytelling

### Bringing the User Experience to Life

**[Storytelling](https://bigbangpartnership.co.uk/business-storytelling-for-innovators-facilitators-change-makers/) is a powerful tool in Design Thinking, enriching our understanding of the user experience. By using narratives and journey mapping, you can create a vivid picture of our users’ experiences and emotions, promoting a deeper level of empathy and understanding.**

In the world of design thinking, storytelling isn’t just an art; it’s an essential tool that brings empathy, understanding, and human connection to the heart of our problem-solving process.

Why is storytelling so crucial? Because it allows us to see through the eyes of our users, to feel their joys and frustrations, and to immerse ourselves in their world. It bridges the gap between us and them, transforming abstract data into tangible, relatable experiences.

So, how can we incorporate storytelling into our design sprints?

In design sprints, storytelling enables us to step into our users’ shoes, to feel their needs and desires, and to design solutions that truly resonate.

## Design Thinking Tip #9: Driving Momentum in Design Sprints

### The Role of Urgency in Design Sprints

**A sense of urgency is a critical factor in the success of a Design Sprint. [Tim Brown](https://www.amazon.com/Change-Design-Transforms-Organizations-Innovation/dp/0061766089) also emphasizes the importance of urgency as a driving force for innovation. Techniques like timeboxing and visual countdowns can create this sense of urgency, maintaining momentum and helping the design team to stay focused and engaged.**

A sense of urgency can be the potent catalyst that propels us from idea generation to innovative solutions. It’s about stoking the fires of momentum, creating a focused, fast-paced atmosphere that fosters creative breakthroughs.

Why is this sense of urgency so beneficial? Because it helps us shake off the shackles of perfectionism, encourages swift decision-making, and maintains a steady rhythm of progress. It’s a race against the clock that stimulates our creative energies and pushes us to think on our feet.

So, how can facilitators foster a sense of urgency?

## Design Thinking Tip #10: Cultivating a Culture of Innovation Through Curiosity

**Curiosity is a powerful engine that drives the Design Thinking process. By asking the right questions and constantly challenging the status quo, design thinkers can stimulate creative problem solving and generate innovative solutions. Encouraging a culture of curiosity is a great way to ensure that new ideas are always on the horizon.**

In the realm of design thinking, curiosity isn’t just welcome; it’s essential. It’s the driving force behind every question asked, every problem explored, and every solution crafted.

Why is a culture of curiosity so crucial? Because it propels us to challenge the status quo, to ask ‘why?’ and ‘what if?’ It encourages us to dive deeper, explore different perspectives, and uncover hidden truths that can lead to breakthrough innovations.

So, how can we foster a culture of curiosity?

In our design thinking sprints, we’re not just problem-solvers; we’re explorers. So, let’s ask, let’s learn, let’s explore.

## Conclusion: The Transformative Power of Design Thinking

Design Thinking, as discussed in the [Design Management Institute](https://www.dmi.org/page/WhatisDesignThink) and highlighted by great leaders like Tim Brown and [Jeanne Liedtka](https://designatdarden.org/app/uploads/2018/01/Working-paper-Liedtka-Evaluating-the-Impact-of-Design-Thinking.pdf), is a journey. It’s an iterative process of understanding the user, defining the problem, ideating possible solutions, creating prototypes, and testing them – all with a deep focus on the human point of view.

It’s a brilliant process, although its complexity can make it challenging at times. These Design Thinking tips will help you navigate successfully through your sprint journey.

In recent years, the use of Design Thinking has gone beyond product development and business strategy. It’s now impacting our personal lives and society at large. It has become a tool for tackling complex problems, fostering a culture of innovation, and creating a positive impact.

These Design Thinking tips, when leveraged correctly, will enable you to innovate design solutions that truly make a difference.

The power of Design Thinking lies in its versatility. Whatever your profession, Design Thinking offers creative approaches. These can help you challenge assumptions, rethink the status quo, and drive innovation.

In our increasingly complex and interconnected world, Design Thinking offers a beacon of clarity. It encourages us to ask the right questions, challenge our preconceptions, and view the world from a different perspective. And in doing so, it empowers us to create solutions that are innovative, effective and deeply human.

Remember, Design Thinking is not the only way to innovate, but it’s a great way to start. So let’s challenge assumptions, think critically, use these Design Thinking tips and put the human at the heart of our design process.
